text: Chapter Eleven L ara entered the shower after leaving the attic, and Ronin banished himself to the lower level, doing all he could to keep from simulating images of her naked body glistening in the water . He moved his bag into the kitchen and unpacked the food, setting it out on the island counter. After refilling her canteen, he carried it to the table, seating himself. He removed the journal from his pocket. A human had penned it, just as Lara said, and Ronin knew whatever story it told would not be comforting to either of them. He brushed his thumb over the cover, but did not open it . After eleven minutes and fourteen seconds, Lara stepped into the kitchen. She wore some of the clothes he’d obtained for her — olive fatigues with patches on the knees and a baggy, off-white wool sweater. Its broad neck sagged off her shoulder. He averted his gaze as she hurried to the waiting food . Ronin watched her eat, thankful for the distraction. The eclectic meal she’d selected rapidly vanished, and he couldn’t look away from the subtle changes flickering over her expression with each new flavor, couldn’t look away from her tongue slipping out to lick her lips clean. She made soft, contented sounds in her throat as she chewed. Even in something as simple as food, she found pleasure. No programming required . “So,” she said around a mouthful of food, “Ronin your real name ?” “It’s the only one I have,” he replied, forcing his optics to meet her eyes . “Did someone give it to you, or did you choose it ?” “I chose it. Twelve years after I was reactivated .” “Twelve years with no name?” She swallowed, ran her tongue over her teeth, and took another bite . Though he was fully aware at the time, those years were still confusing to him, the memories somehow disorienting. Despite all the data he’d accumulated since, his recollection of that period was forever tainted by the memory of how little he understood at the time. He’d known the world was broken, that everything was wrong, but couldn’t determine how or why . “I didn’t need a name to identify myself .” “But you gave yourself one, anyway .” “For the benefit of others. Easier than saying that bot or hey you .” “Then why does everyone around here call you dustwalker ?” “Because there are few who choose to do as I do. It’s an easily identifiable title, and there are rarely more than one or two successful dustwalkers in any given town at any time. We’re…respected. But also, a little feared. Always…on the outside .” “Why is that?” Lara folded a slice of meat and laid it on her waiting tongue . “Bots operate on logic. Constantly assessing risk ver — ” “Isn’t it more logical not to go into the Dust ?” Ronin tilted his mouth into a smirk. “Yes. But settlements need raw materials to continue to produce the parts that bots need to function. Someone has to go out and find them .” “Why do you ?” “Should I be making a list of these questions so I can eventually answer them all, or do you just lose interest if my answer is more than a few words long ?” She chewed slowly, eyes wide as she stared at him. Seconds ticked by. The only sounds were Lara’s mastication and the refrigerator’s soft hum . “Getting a little irritated there, dustwalker ?” “I prefer you call me Ronin,” he replied . “ Why ?” He couldn’t tell for sure, but there seemed to be a hint of humor in her voice . “I think I’m through answering questions for now, human .” “Point taken .” “May I get back to what I was saying ?” She picked up the canteen, unscrewed the cap, and took a drink. Seeing her use it made him feel something close to satisfaction. Wiping her mouth with the back of her hand, she nodded . “You asked why there are so few dustwalkers. It’s because most who take up the calling are ended in the Dust. Whether a walker is a human or a bot, nobody goes to look for them when they don’t return .” The motion of her jaw slowed. “And…you go out there by choice ?” “Yes.” Because he was compelled to . “ Why ?” “Perhaps the risk-calculation function of my processors was damaged in the Black Out .” “But you know the risks. You said you were trying to survive, just like me. How is putting yourself in danger like that survival ?” Ronin lifted a hand and scratched his cheek. Touching it with something other than the bare metal of his fingers was nearly as strange as the impulse to scratch in the first place . “It’s survival because I’m still moving,” he finally said. “Existence is a constant fight, and I choose to seek that fight on my own terms. If I were to stay in a place like this all the time…at some point, I’d sit down and never get up again .” “Because you feel like you have no purpose .” Her words flared across his processors like a physical blow. A second passed; three seconds; fifteen. It was half a minute before he formulated a response . “I never said that .” “You didn’t have to.” She slid the food wrapper away, a half a strip of meat and three slices of carrot still atop it. “Why’d you pick Ronin ?” Electrodes crackled across his cheek again, demanding physical stimulation, but he kept his hand down. Was he so easily assessed? After all this time, was he so easy to read ? “In the twelfth year after I was awoken, I found a book. There were many more of them, back then. This one was about a place called Japan, which must have existed centuries ago. It
